name: Caro-Kann Defense
description: A solid, reliable defense against 1.e4. Black prepares d5 without locking in the light-squared bishop, avoiding the main drawback of the French Defense.

moves.commentary: King's pawn opening.
The Caro-Kann — prepares d5 while keeping the light-squared bishop free to develop. Solid and reliable.
White claims the center with two pawns.
Challenges e4. Unlike the French, the bishop on c8 isn't blocked.
Defends e4 with the knight.
Black captures, relieving the central tension.
Recaptures with the knight. White has a strong centralized piece.
The Classical — develops the bishop outside the pawn chain immediately. This is why Black played c6 instead of e6.
Attacks the bishop, forcing it to decide.
Retreats to g6, maintaining the bishop on a decent diagonal.
Gains space on the kingside and threatens h5 to trap the bishop.
Prevents h5 and gives the bishop an escape route on h7.
Develops the second knight. White has a pleasant space advantage.
Develops the knight to d7, preparing Ngf6 and e6 for a solid setup.
Offers to trade the light-squared bishops. White aims for a clear development advantage.
King's pawn.
Caro-Kann.
Full center.
Challenges e4.
The Advance — White pushes forward, gaining space but committing the pawn structure early.
Develops the bishop outside the chain before playing e6. Key Caro-Kann idea.
Develops and supports the center.
Solidifies the pawn chain and prepares to break with c5.
Develops and prepares to castle.
Attacks the base of White's pawn chain — the standard break.
Develops and supports d4.
Develops toward c5 or supports a future f6 break against e5.
White castles, completing development.
Develops the knight with flexibility — can go to f5, g6, or c6.
King's pawn.
Caro-Kann.
Full center.
Challenges e4.
The Exchange — White simplifies the center. Considered less ambitious but leads to clear plans.
Recaptures, opening the c-file. The position is symmetrical but White has a slight initiative.
Develops the bishop actively, targeting the kingside.
Develops and pressures d4.
Supports d4 and prevents Nb4 attacking the bishop.
Develops and controls the center.
Develops outside the pawn chain. White aims for a minority attack on the queenside.
Pins the knight on f3 (once it develops) and develops actively.
Attacks b7 and d5. White tries to exploit the slightly open position.
Defends b7 and connects the rooks. A solid response.
King's pawn.
Caro-Kann.
Full center.
Challenges e4.
Exchanges.
Recaptures with the c-pawn.
The Panov-Botvinnik! Immediately attacks Black's d5 pawn. Transforms into an IQP (Isolated Queen Pawn) position.
Develops and defends d5.
Develops and attacks d5 again.
Solidifies d5 and opens a line for the bishop.
Develops the last minor piece.
Develops and prepares to castle.
Creates the IQP on d4. White gets active piece play; Black targets the isolated pawn.
Recaptures with the knight, occupying a strong central post.
Develops and aims at the kingside. Typical IQP play — White attacks, Black blockades.
